region_of_origin: China; widely adopted in Singapore as a traditional Chinese-Singaporean festival dessert
food_subgroup: Glutinous rice flour balls filled with black sesame paste, served in sweet ginger-rock sugar broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific concept for tang yuan (glutinous rice balls in sweet broth). Closest parent concept is 'glutinous rice dumpling in sweet broth'. No exact URI available; null assigned pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite food — sweet glutinous rice dumpling in syrup broth; mapped to FoodEx2 group A000E (Composite dishes — sweet) as closest defensible parent. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for tang yuan.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for tang yuan. A000E (Composite dishes) used as closest defensible parent. Flagged for FoodEx2 expert review.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for tang yuan or glutinous rice balls in sweet broth as of April 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 8 small black sesame-filled glutinous rice balls (approximately 150g) served in approximately 100ml sweet ginger-rock sugar broth, totalling approximately 250g.

nutrient_source.title: Tang Yuan Black Sesame — HPB Singapore Foodo (component analysis)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Foodo database and component analysis
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database component analysis for black sesame tang yuan. Values represent the composite dish (glutinous rice flour skin + black sesame paste filling + sweet ginger broth) expressed per 100g of the combined serving. All core macros and secondary nutrients confirmed from this source. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Glutinous Rice and Rice-Based Products — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for black sesame-filled tang yuan in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~70 (high) based on: (1) glutinous rice flour skin GI ~80–85 per University of Sydney GI Database (glutinous rice, GI=98 boiled; sticky rice products range 70–98); (2) fat and protein content of black sesame paste moderates overall GI downward from the skin-only value; (3) composite GI estimated at ~70 using weighted-component method. Glycemic load per 250g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (70 × 70) / 100 = 49.0. Carbohydrate per 250g serving = 28.0 × 2.5 = 70g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (10mg/100g; ~25mg per 250g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Tang yuan with black sesame paste is very low in sodium (~25mg per standard 250g serving), making it suitable for individuals managing hypertension, provided the sweet ginger broth is prepared without added salt or soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load per serving (GL ~49) driven by glutinous rice flour skin (GI ~70–80)
type2_diabetes.guidance: A standard 8-ball serving (250g) delivers approximately 70g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~49 — equivalent to more than two bowls of white rice glycemically — and is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; a tasting portion of 2–3 balls (GL ~12–18) is the maximum clinically advisable amount, and blood glucose monitoring after consumption is strongly recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (1.8g/100g) and cholesterol (15mg/100g); beneficial sesamin lignans and low sodium partially offset risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tang yuan with black sesame paste contains moderate saturated fat (1.8g/100g; ~4.5g per serving) and cholesterol (~37.5mg per serving), which warrants portion control for individuals with cardiovascular disease; however, the black sesame filling provides sesamin and sesamolin lignans with antioxidant properties, and the very low sodium content is cardioprotective.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory compounds from black sesame (sesamin, sesamolin) and ginger broth (gingerols, shogaols)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Black sesame filling provides sesamin and sesamolin lignans with documented antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, and the ginger broth contributes gingerols and shogaols with COX-inhibitory activity; however, the high glycemic load of the glutinous rice skin may promote systemic inflammation at full serving size, so portion moderation is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tang Yuan (汤圆 / 湯圓, literally 'soup balls') with black sesame filling are small, smooth, slightly chewy balls made from glutinous rice flour dough, each filled with a rich, intensely nutty sweetened black sesame paste, and served floating in a warm, fragrant sweet ginger-rock sugar broth. They are the quintessential dessert of the Chinese Winter Solstice festival (Dongzhi, 冬至) and the Lantern Festival (元宵节), eaten to symbolise family togetherness — the round shape and the name 'tang yuan' (汤圆) share a phonetic resonance with 'tuán yuán' (团圆, family reunion). In Singapore, black sesame is the most popular filling variant. Nutritionally, the dish provides 155 kcal per 100g, with 28g carbohydrate, 4.5g fat, and 2.2g protein. The black sesame filling contributes meaningful calcium (~55mg/100g composite), iron (1.5mg), zinc (0.8mg), magnesium (22mg), and sesamin/sesamolin lignans with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. The warm ginger broth contributes gingerols and shogaols with COX-inhibitory anti-inflammatory activity. However, the glutinous rice flour skin carries a very high glycemic index (~70–80), and a standard 8-ball serving (250g) delivers a glycemic load of approximately 49 — equivalent to more than two bowls of white rice — making this a high-glycemic event that requires strict portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium is very low (10mg/100g), making it suitable for hypertension management. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo component analysis for black sesame tang yuan, which is an adapted composite analysis rather than a direct laboratory measurement of a single standardised product. GI is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database, with fat/protein moderation applied. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for black sesame tang yuan composite dish not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish (balls + broth) not reported in HPB Foodo source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no vitamin C-containing ingredients in this dish). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — this is a plant-based vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no vitamin D-containing ingredients). GI of 70 is an analogous estimate: glutinous rice flour skin GI ~80–85 (University of Sydney GI Database); composite GI moderated to ~70 by fat and protein content of black sesame filling. Glycemic load per 250g serving = (70 × 70) / 100 = 49.0 (carbohydrate per 250g = 70g). Two default portions exist in source data: 200g (6 pieces + broth) and 250g (8 balls + broth); 250g used as primary serving size per dish name specification. Cholesterol value of 15mg/100g is noted — traditional tang yuan with black sesame paste is vegan; this value may reflect recipe variants using lard in the filling; the dietary flag is_vegan is set to true for the standard recipe but consumers should verify filling ingredients. FoodEx2 code A000E assigned as closest defensible parent for composite sweet dish; flagged for expert review.
